summary: Normal haemoglobin in adult males is 14-18 g/dL and in adult females is 12-16 g/dL.
Normal haemoglobin in adult males is 10-12 g/dL and in adult females is 8-10 g/dL.
Haemoglobin levels are the same in males and females at 14-16 g/dL.
Erythropoietin is produced mainly by the kidneys in response to hypoxia and stimulates erythropoiesis.
Erythropoietin is produced by the liver in response to hyperoxia.
Erythropoietin is produced by the bone marrow and inhibits red blood cell production.
The intrinsic pathway is activated by contact with exposed collagen and is measured by the aPTT test.
The intrinsic pathway is activated by tissue factor and is measured by the PT test.
The intrinsic pathway is activated by calcium ions and measured by bleeding time.
ABO blood group antigens are on the red cell surface, and the corresponding antibodies are in the plasma.
ABO blood group antibodies are on the red cell surface, and the antigens are in the plasma.
Both ABO antigens and antibodies are found in the plasma only.
Iron is absorbed mainly in the duodenum and transported in blood bound to transferrin.
Iron is absorbed mainly in the ileum and transported bound to ferritin.
Iron is absorbed in the colon and transported bound to albumin.
Mature red blood cells are biconcave discs that lack a nucleus and have a lifespan of approximately 120 days.
Mature red blood cells are spherical, contain a nucleus, and live for approximately 30 days.
Mature red blood cells are biconcave but retain their nucleus throughout their lifespan of 60 days.
Group O individuals are universal donors because their red cells lack A and B antigens.
Group AB individuals are universal donors because they have both A and B antigens.
Group A individuals are universal donors because they lack B antigens.

overallFeedback.feedback: Good effort! Review the areas you missed.
